manifest.description: Canonical mapping of SQL Sentry condition names to Redgate Monitor alert type IDs, with severity consolidation and migration strategy notes.

severityMapping.strategy: SQL Sentry uses an integer scale (0–3) internally. Redgate Monitor exposes three named threshold bands rendered as Blue (Low), Amber (Medium), and Red (High). Severity 0 is informational and collapses to Low; severity 1 remains Low; severity 2 maps to Medium; severity 3 maps to High. Advisory best-practice conditions that are not real-time performance emergencies are intentionally downgraded one level from their SQL Sentry default so that Red bandwidth is reserved for active incidents.
severityMapping.advisoryDowngradeRule: Advisory / best-practice conditions (index health, configuration drift, VLF count, compatibility level) default to Low even when SQL Sentry marks them Medium. Escalate only when the condition has persisted beyond an agreed remediation window.

phases.name: Direct Replacements
phases.description: Conditions with a 1-to-1 built-in Redgate Monitor alert type. Enable immediately — no custom metric setup required.

phases.name: Partial / Configured Replacements
phases.description: Built-in Redgate Monitor alerts that cover the spirit of the SQL Sentry condition but require threshold tuning or supplemental Extended Events / error log monitoring.
conditions: High Percentage CPU for Single Core

phases.name: Custom Metrics
phases.description: No built-in Redgate Monitor equivalent. Build a Custom Metric using the provided T-SQL query for each condition. Group related DMV queries into a single custom metric where possible to reduce alert noise.

conditions: High Average Wait Time Per User Session
SQL Server Process Paged to Disk
High Ad Hoc Query Plans in Cache
Optimize For Ad Hoc Workloads Changed
Sleeping Session With Long Open Transaction
TempDB Data Files Suboptimal Count (<=8 cores)
Suspect Pages Increase in Fixed Pages
Trace Flags Number Turned On Changed
SQL Server Process Physical Memory Low
SQL Server Process Virtual Memory Low
VMware High Ready Time % per vCPU
VMware High Ready Time % per vCPU Warning

phases.name: Not Available — Decommission or Replace
phases.description: Conditions that require VMware/Hyper-V host inventory APIs, WMI power plan settings, or SQL Sentry internal state with no T-SQL equivalent and no viable proxy. VMware performance counters (ballooning, co-stop, ready time), Hyper-V vCPU wait time, and Tintri storage counters DO have T-SQL runnable-tasks / I/O-latency proxies — see phase 3 partial conditions. Only conditions listed here have no practical Redgate Monitor equivalent.

alternatives.condition: Availability Replicas Hosted on Same Virtual Host
alternatives.alternative: Use VMware vRealize or Hyper-V SCVMM placement policies.

alternatives.condition: Availability Replicas With Disks in Same Datastore
alternatives.alternative: Use VMware vRealize datastore anti-affinity rules.

alternatives.condition: High Performance Power Plan Not Enabled
alternatives.alternative: Enforce via Group Policy (PowerCfg) or DSC on all SQL Server hosts.
Redgate Monitor has built-in agent health monitoring — no equivalent condition needed.

conditions.migrationNote: Machine-level CPU threshold. Enable in Redgate Monitor > Alert Settings. Recommended threshold: Low=70%, Medium=85%, High=95%.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Use the built-in alert. Disable the SQL Sentry condition after RG alert is validated.

conditions.migrationNote: Machine-level available RAM threshold. Maps directly to Redgate Monitor 'OS memory available' alert.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Direct replacement. Set thresholds to match your SQL Server max memory headroom.

conditions.migrationNote: Latency benchmarks: <10ms=fast, 10–20ms=acceptable, 20–50ms=slow, >50ms=critical. Transaction logs should stay under 2ms.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Direct replacement. Tune Low/Medium/High thresholds to match your storage tier SLAs.

conditions.migrationNote: Disabled by default in Redgate Monitor v14+. Enable under Alert Settings > Resources > Network.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Direct replacement once the alert is enabled.

conditions.migrationNote: Instance connectivity and service check. Built in to Redgate Monitor — always enabled.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Direct replacement.

conditions.migrationNote: Head-blocker wait time threshold. Redgate Monitor also surfaces a live blocking tree.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Direct replacement. Recommend Low=30s, Medium=60s, High=120s.

conditions.migrationNote: Event alert with deadlock graph captured via Extended Events session.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Direct replacement. Redgate Monitor's deadlock alert captures the full deadlock graph.

conditions.migrationNote: Based on msdb.dbo.backupset backup_finish_date vs configured interval.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Direct replacement. Configure separate thresholds for FULL, DIFF, and LOG backup intervals.

conditions.migrationNote: Reads msdb.dbo.sysjobhistory for run_status = 0 (failed).
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Direct replacement.

conditions.migrationNote: Machine-level Disk Used % or GB free threshold.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Direct replacement. Set both a % and an absolute GB threshold so large drives still alert.

conditions.ssConditionName: AG / Cluster Failover / Mirroring State Change
conditions.migrationNote: Redgate Monitor's AG / Cluster failover alert covers actual failover events and role changes (primary→secondary) directly. However, mirroring state changes are a partial match only — database mirroring is deprecated since SQL Server 2012 and Redgate Monitor treats these as connectivity events rather than mirroring-specific state transitions. For AG health degradation (replica synchronization, send queue growth, redo queue buildup) use custom metrics on sys.dm_hadr_database_replica_states — these are NOT covered by the AG / Cluster failover alert and must not be confused with topology-change events.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Direct replacement for AG failover and Windows cluster role changes. Partial for mirroring (deprecated). Build a separate custom metric for AG sync health — do not rely on the failover alert to detect health degradation.

conditions.migrationNote: Elapsed time threshold. Redgate Monitor default is 90s. Tune per workload.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Direct replacement.

conditions.migrationNote: Behavior differs: SQL Sentry alerts on any open transaction regardless of current query activity, including sleeping sessions holding open transactions. Redgate Monitor's Long running query alert requires an active query in the session. Sleeping sessions with open transactions (the most dangerous case) require a custom metric: SELECT s.session_id, DATEDIFF(MINUTE,t.transaction_begin_time,GETDATE()) AS open_minutes FROM sys.dm_exec_sessions s JOIN sys.dm_tran_session_transactions t ON s.session_id=t.session_id WHERE s.status='sleeping' AND DATEDIFF(MINUTE,t.transaction_begin_time,GETDATE()) > 10. Also enable the Blocking processes alert — open transactions prevent log truncation, grow tempdb under RCSI, and escalate locks.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Partial replacement via Long running query. Add a custom metric for sleeping sessions with open transactions. Pair with Blocking processes alert.

conditions.migrationNote: Monitor SQL error log for 'Login failed' messages. Supplement with Extended Events audit session for detailed attribution.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Enable SQL Server log entry alert with keyword filter 'Login failed'.

conditions.migrationNote: Configuration change detection. Log entry alert or supplemental Extended Events. Recommended baseline: MAXDOP=logical_cores/2 (max 8), CTP=50.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Use SQL Server log entry alert or consolidate into the Configuration Drift custom metric group.

conditions.ssConditionName: Network Bottleneck — Outbound Output Queue Length
conditions.migrationNote: Output Queue Length > 3 requires WMI/OS-level monitoring. Redgate Monitor Network utilization is the closest built-in equivalent.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Use Network utilization alert as proxy. For precise output queue length alerting, use OS monitoring (PRTG, Datadog, etc.).

conditions.ssConditionName: High Percentage CPU for Single Core Saturation
conditions.migrationNote: Redgate Monitor alerts on overall CPU%. Per-core saturation proxy: threshold = 100 / logical_core_count. E.g. on a 16-core server, one saturated core = ~6% total.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Use CPU utilization alert with a low-% threshold as a proxy. Per-core granularity requires OS-level monitoring.

conditions.ssConditionName: Availability Replicas Have Send or Recovery Queue Exceeding Threshold
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT MAX(CASE WHEN send_queue_size > 3072 OR redo_queue_size > 3072 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS queue_breached FROM sys.dm_hadr_database_replica_states WHERE is_local = 0
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when result = 1 (3 MB / 3072 KB default)
conditions.migrationNote: Part of Scheduler Health consolidation group. Alert when either send or redo queue exceeds 3 MB.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate with Mirroring Queue metric into a single 'Replication Queue' custom metric.

conditions.ssConditionName: Mirrored Databases Have Send or Recovery Queue Exceeding Threshold
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT MAX(CASE WHEN mirroring_send_queue_size > 3072 OR mirroring_redo_queue_size > 3072 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS queue_breached FROM sys.database_mirroring WHERE mirroring_guid IS NOT NULL
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when result = 1
conditions.migrationNote: Mirror is deprecated in SQL 2022. Prioritise AG queue metric if on modern SQL Server.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate with AG Replica Queue into a single 'Replication Queue' custom metric group.

conditions.ssConditionName: Availability Replicas Hosted on Same Virtual Host
conditions.migrationNote: Requires VMware/Hyper-V host inventory API — no T-SQL equivalent.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Use VMware vRealize anti-affinity rules or Hyper-V SCVMM placement policies instead.

conditions.ssConditionName: Availability Replicas With Disks in Same Datastore
conditions.migrationNote: Requires VMware datastore inventory — no T-SQL equivalent.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Enforce via VMware datastore anti-affinity rules. Accept the monitoring gap in Redgate Monitor.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS untrusted_checks FROM sys.check_constraints WHERE is_not_trusted = 1 AND is_disabled = 0
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0
conditions.migrationNote: Untrusted constraints prevent the query optimiser from using constraint elimination. Run WITH CHECK CHECK CONSTRAINT ALL on offending tables.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Index / Constraint Health' custom metric group. Low severity — best practice, not an emergency.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS untrusted_fks FROM sys.foreign_keys WHERE is_not_trusted = 1 AND is_disabled = 0
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0
conditions.migrationNote: Untrusted FKs are usually caused by bulk inserts with NOCHECK or restores. Run ALTER TABLE ... WITH CHECK CHECK CONSTRAINT to re-trust.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Index / Constraint Health' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS non_checksum_dbs FROM sys.databases WHERE page_verify_option_desc <> 'CHECKSUM' AND state_desc = 'ONLINE' AND name NOT IN ('tempdb')
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0
conditions.migrationNote: CHECKSUM is the only page verification option that detects I/O corruption. TORN_PAGE_DETECTION catches only partial writes.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Index / Constraint Health' custom metric group. Medium severity due to data integrity risk.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT CAST(value_in_use AS INT) AS current_ctp FROM sys.configurations WHERE name = 'cost threshold for parallelism'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when value differs from your agreed baseline (recommended baseline: 50)
conditions.migrationNote: Store the expected value in the threshold. Alert on deviation rather than an absolute value.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate all three configuration change conditions (CTP, MAXDOP, ad hoc workloads) into a single 'Configuration Drift' custom metric.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT CAST(value_in_use AS INT) AS current_maxdop FROM sys.configurations WHERE name = 'max degree of parallelism'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when value differs from your agreed baseline
conditions.migrationNote: Recommended: MAXDOP = min(logical_cores / 2, 8) for OLTP. Alert on any deviation.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Configuration Drift' custom metric group.

conditions.ssConditionName: Optimize for Ad Hoc Workloads Changed
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT CAST(value_in_use AS INT) AS ad_hoc_enabled FROM sys.configurations WHERE name = 'optimize for ad hoc workloads'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when = 0 (should always be enabled on OLTP servers)
conditions.migrationNote: Should be enabled on all OLTP instances to avoid single-use plan cache bloat.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Configuration Drift' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS failed_workers FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WHERE failed_to_create_worker = 1
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert immediately when > 0
conditions.migrationNote: Typically caused by memory pressure preventing new worker thread creation. Immediate action required.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Part of 'Scheduler Health' consolidation group. Keep as High severity — this is a critical condition.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS HotAddedSchedulers FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WITH (NOLOCK) WHERE [status] = N'HOT_ADDED'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0 (informational — CPU was added dynamically)
conditions.migrationNote: Detects dynamic CPU expansion. Informational — validate that SQL Server is correctly using the new scheduler. Note: status value is 'HOT_ADDED' (underscore, no space) — the original manifest had an incorrect LIKE filter.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Scheduler Health' custom metric group. Low severity.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS visible_offline FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WHERE is_online = 0 AND scheduler_id < 255 AND status NOT LIKE '%HIDDEN%'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0
conditions.migrationNote: Offline CPUs cause NUMA imbalance and licensing waste. Check affinity mask settings.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Scheduler Health' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS unused_schedulers FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WHERE is_online = 0 AND scheduler_id < 255
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0 (review if intentional via affinity mask)
conditions.migrationNote: Caused by affinity masking or licensing restrictions. Validate that offline schedulers are intentional.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Scheduler Health' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT CAST(SUM(signal_wait_time_ms) AS FLOAT) / NULLIF(SUM(wait_time_ms), 0) * 100 AS signal_wait_pct FROM sys.dm_os_wait_stats WHERE wait_type NOT IN ('SLEEP_TASK','SQLTRACE_BUFFER_FLUSH','WAITFOR','BROKER_TO_FLUSH','BROKER_EVENTHANDLER','CHECKPOINT_QUEUE','DBMIRROR_EVENTS_QUEUE','SQLTRACE_INCREMENTAL_FLUSH_SLEEP','SLEEP_DBSTARTUP','SLEEP_DBRECOVER','SLEEP_MASTERDBREADY','SLEEP_MASTERMDREADY','SLEEP_MASTERUPGRADED','SLEEP_MSDBSTARTUP','SLEEP_SYSTEMTASK','SLEEP_TEMPDBSTARTUP','SNI_HTTP_ACCEPT','SP_SERVER_DIAGNOSTICS_SLEEP','SQLTRACE_BUFFER_FLUSH','WAIT_XTP_OFFLINE_CKPT_NEW_LOG')
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 20%
conditions.migrationNote: High signal waits indicate CPU pressure — tasks are ready to run but waiting for a scheduler. Correlates with runnable task count.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Wait Analysis' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT c.cntr_value AS compilations_per_sec, b.cntr_value AS batches_per_sec, CASE WHEN b.cntr_value > 0 THEN CAST(c.cntr_value AS FLOAT)/b.cntr_value*100 ELSE 0 END AS compile_ratio_pct FROM sys.dm_os_performance_counters c CROSS JOIN sys.dm_os_performance_counters b WHERE c.counter_name = 'SQL Compilations/sec' AND c.object_name LIKE '%SQL Statistics%' AND b.counter_name = 'Batch Requests/sec' AND b.object_name LIKE '%SQL Statistics%'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when compile_ratio_pct > 15%
conditions.migrationNote: High compile ratio indicates poor plan reuse — check for parameter sniffing, missing sp_executesql, or frequent ad-hoc SQL.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Compilation Rates' custom metric group with recompilations.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT cntr_value AS recompilations_per_sec FROM sys.dm_os_performance_counters WHERE counter_name = 'SQL Re-Compilations/sec' AND object_name LIKE '%SQL Statistics%'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 15% of initial compilations/sec
conditions.migrationNote: Frequent recompilations are caused by schema changes, SET option changes, or statistics updates mid-execution.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Compilation Rates' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT AVG(runnable_tasks_count) AS avg_runnable FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WHERE scheduler_id < 255 AND is_online = 1
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when avg > 10 (strong CPU pressure indicator)
conditions.migrationNote: avg_runnable_tasks > 10 sustained is a reliable indicator of CPU saturation. Correlates with Signal Wait % alert.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Scheduler Health' custom metric group. Keep High severity.

conditions.ssConditionName: High Percentage CPU for Non-SQL Server Process
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT TOP 1 (100 - record.value('(./Record/SchedulerMonitorEvent/SystemHealth/SystemIdle)[1]', 'INT') - record.value('(./Record/SchedulerMonitorEvent/SystemHealth/ProcessUtilization)[1]', 'INT')) AS NonSqlCpuPercent FROM (SELECT CAST(record AS XML) AS record FROM sys.dm_os_ring_buffers WHERE ring_buffer_type = N'RING_BUFFER_SCHEDULER_MONITOR' AND record LIKE N'%<SystemHealth>%') AS rb ORDER BY record.value('(./Record/@id)[1]', 'BIGINT') DESC
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 15% (Low), > 25% (Medium), > 40% (High) sustained
conditions.migrationNote: Full per-process CPU attribution requires WMI. T-SQL proxy subtracts SQL Server process CPU and system idle from 100 using ring buffer data. Result can be negative if ring buffer is stale — add CASE WHEN result < 0 THEN 0 guard in production. Previous manifest entry incorrectly used Resource Pool Stats (default pool only, not total SQL CPU).
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Scheduler Health' group or use as a supplemental OS alert. Medium severity.

conditions.migrationNote: Maps directly to Redgate Monitor avg read-write time alert. Benchmarks: <10ms fast, 10–20ms acceptable, 20–50ms slow, >50ms critical.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Direct replacement. Set Low=20ms, Medium=35ms, High=50ms as starting thresholds.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT SUM(pending_disk_io_count) AS total_pending_io FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WHERE scheduler_id < 255
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0 sustained (any pending I/O indicates I/O subsystem saturation)
conditions.migrationNote: Sustained pending I/O is a critical indicator — the I/O subsystem cannot keep up with demand.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Standalone metric. High severity. Pair with avg read-write time alert for full I/O picture.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT ISNULL(MAX(vlf_count), 0) AS MaxVlfCount FROM (SELECT COUNT(*) AS vlf_count FROM sys.dm_db_log_info(NULL) GROUP BY database_id) AS vlf
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 1000 VLFs across any database
conditions.migrationNote: High VLF count slows log backups and recovery. Fix: backup the log, shrink to minimum, then resize with one large growth increment (e.g. 4 GB in a single step). Previous manifest entry used DB_ID()/DB_NAME() context functions which only checked master — corrected to use sys.dm_db_log_info(NULL) to scan all databases (SQL Server 2016+).
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'File & Storage' custom metric group. Low severity — maintenance issue, not a real-time emergency.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT name, DB_NAME(database_id) AS db_name, size * 8 / 1024 AS size_mb FROM sys.master_files
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when size_mb decreases vs prior snapshot (compare current vs stored baseline)
conditions.migrationNote: Data file shrinks cause index fragmentation and log file VLF proliferation. Almost always harmful — investigate the root cause immediately.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'File & Storage' custom metric group. High severity — shrink events are always actionable.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT DB_NAME(database_id) AS db_name, type_desc, COUNT(*) AS file_count FROM sys.master_files GROUP BY database_id, type_desc
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when count differs from stored baseline
conditions.migrationNote: Unexpected file count changes should trigger a backup job review. New files added outside change control are a DBA concern.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'File & Storage' custom metric group. Low severity — informational.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT name, DB_NAME(database_id) AS db_name, physical_name FROM sys.master_files WHERE is_percent_growth = 0 AND growth = 0
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when any row returned
conditions.migrationNote: Disabled auto-growth with no fixed growth means DML will halt when space is exhausted. Enable auto-growth as a safety net (even if you pre-size files).
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'File & Storage' custom metric group. High severity — can cause complete DML halt.

conditions.ssConditionName: Auto-Growth Next Event Exceeds Free Disk Space
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS FilesAtRiskOfGrowthFailure FROM sys.master_files AS mf CROSS APPLY sys.dm_os_volume_stats(mf.database_id, mf.file_id) AS vs WHERE mf.growth > 0 AND mf.state_desc = N'ONLINE' AND ((mf.is_percent_growth = 0 AND (CAST(mf.growth AS BIGINT) * 8192) > (vs.available_bytes * 0.5)) OR (mf.max_size > 0 AND (mf.size + mf.growth) > mf.max_size))
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0 (any file where next auto-growth may fail)
conditions.migrationNote: The next auto-growth event would exceed available disk space or the configured max_size. Uses sys.dm_os_volume_stats (SQL Server 2008 R2+) to check actual available bytes on the volume. Previous manifest entry incorrectly calculated against max_size only — corrected to use real free space via dm_os_volume_stats.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'File & Storage' custom metric group. High severity — pre-emptive disk space alert.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT cntr_value AS ple FROM sys.dm_os_performance_counters WHERE counter_name = 'Page life expectancy' AND object_name LIKE '%Buffer Manager%'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when PLE < (buffer_pool_GB / 4) * 300. E.g. 16 GB buffer pool = alert at < 1200s
conditions.migrationNote: PLE dropping rapidly indicates buffer pool churn. Check per NUMA node via '%Buffer Node%' object_name for multi-NUMA servers.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Memory Pressure' custom metric group. High severity.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT cntr_value AS memory_grants_pending FROM sys.dm_os_performance_counters WHERE counter_name = 'Memory Grants Pending' AND object_name LIKE '%Memory Manager%'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert immediately when > 0 (should always be 0)
conditions.migrationNote: Any pending memory grants indicate RESOURCE_SEMAPHORE waits. Correlates directly with query throughput degradation.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Memory Pressure' custom metric group. High severity — alert threshold is > 0.

conditions.ssConditionName: SQL Server Process Paged to Disk
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT physical_memory_in_use_kb, committed_target_kb, CAST(physical_memory_in_use_kb AS FLOAT) / NULLIF(committed_target_kb, 0) AS memory_ratio FROM sys.dm_os_process_memory
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when memory_ratio < 0.75 (SQL Server using less than 75% of its committed target — indicates OS paging)
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Server being paged to disk is a critical performance emergency. Lock pages in memory (LPIM) should be enabled on production SQL Servers.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Memory Pressure' custom metric group. High severity — critical condition.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT system_cache_kb, available_physical_memory_kb, total_physical_memory_kb, CAST(system_cache_kb AS FLOAT)/total_physical_memory_kb*100 AS cache_pct FROM sys.dm_os_sys_memory
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when cache_pct > 50% AND available_physical_memory_kb < 524288 (< 512 MB free)
conditions.migrationNote: Critical in SSAS or mixed-workload environments. Windows file cache can starve SQL Server of memory if LPIM is not enabled.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Memory Pressure' custom metric group. Medium severity.

conditions.ssConditionName: High Ad Hoc Query Plans in Cache
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) * 8 / 1024 AS adhoc_cache_mb, (SELECT SUM(size_in_bytes)/1024/1024 FROM sys.dm_exec_cached_plans) AS total_cache_mb FROM sys.dm_exec_cached_plans WHERE usecounts = 1 AND objtype = 'Adhoc'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when adhoc_cache_mb > 10% of total cache. For servers with >64 GB RAM use 5%.
conditions.migrationNote: Fix by enabling 'optimize for ad hoc workloads'. Single-use plans bloat the plan cache and cause PLE drops under memory pressure.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Memory Pressure' custom metric group. Pair with 'Optimize for Ad Hoc Workloads' configuration check.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT AVG(work_queue_count) AS avg_work_queue FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WHERE scheduler_id < 255 AND is_online = 1
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when avg > 1
conditions.migrationNote: Work queue > 1 means threads are waiting for available workers. Investigate max worker threads setting.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Scheduler Health' custom metric group.

conditions.ssConditionName: High Average Wait Time Per User Session
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT SUM(r.wait_time) / NULLIF(COUNT(*), 0) AS avg_wait_ms_per_session FROM sys.dm_exec_sessions s JOIN sys.dm_exec_requests r ON s.session_id = r.session_id WHERE s.is_user_process = 1
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when avg_wait_ms_per_session > 50
conditions.migrationNote: Sustained high average wait per user session indicates system-wide resource contention. Correlate with wait type breakdown.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Wait Analysis' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS active_user_sessions FROM sys.dm_exec_sessions WHERE is_user_process = 1 AND last_request_start_time > DATEADD(MINUTE, -15, GETDATE())
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Set threshold to match your workload baseline (high session count alone does not indicate a problem)
conditions.migrationNote: Informational — use as a capacity planning indicator rather than a reactive alert.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Session Activity' custom metric group. Low severity.

conditions.customMetricSQL: DECLARE @sql NVARCHAR(MAX) = N''; SELECT @sql = @sql + N' UNION ALL SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ' + QUOTENAME(name) + N'.sys.service_queues WHERE activation_procedure IS NOT NULL AND is_activation_enabled = 0 AND is_receive_enabled = 1;' FROM sys.databases WHERE state_desc = N'ONLINE' AND database_id > 4; SET @sql = STUFF(@sql, 1, 12, N''); CREATE TABLE #sb (cnt INT); INSERT INTO #sb EXEC sys.sp_executesql @sql; SELECT ISNULL(SUM(cnt), 0) AS ActivationDisabledWithProcedure FROM #sb; DROP TABLE #sb;
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0 (queue has an activation procedure but activation is disabled — Service Broker auto-disables activation after 5 consecutive stored procedure failures)
conditions.migrationNote: Activation errors mean the stored procedure triggered by the queue is failing. Service Broker auto-disables activation after 5 consecutive SP failures. Previous manifest entry used sys.dm_broker_activated_tasks WHERE spid IS NULL which shows currently-launching tasks, not failed activation state. Corrected to query sys.service_queues across all user databases for queues with activation procedure but activation disabled.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate all 6 Service Broker conditions into a single 'Service Broker Health' custom metric.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS login_errors FROM sys.dm_broker_connections WHERE login_state = 13
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0 (login_state 13 = connection error)
conditions.migrationNote: Login state 13 indicates a transport-layer authentication failure in the Service Broker endpoint.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Service Broker Health' custom metric group. High severity.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS queues_without_poison_handling FROM sys.service_queues WHERE is_poison_message_handling_enabled = 0
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0
conditions.migrationNote: A poison message can halt all queue processing indefinitely. Poison handling must always be enabled.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Service Broker Health' custom metric group. High severity.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS queues_with_issues FROM sys.service_queues WHERE activation_procedure IS NOT NULL AND (is_activation_enabled = 0 OR is_receive_enabled = 0 OR is_enqueue_enabled = 0)
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0
conditions.migrationNote: Queue with activation procedure but disabled receive/enqueue will silently drop messages.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Service Broker Health' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT cntr_value AS task_limit_reached_per_sec FROM sys.dm_os_performance_counters WHERE counter_name = 'Task Limit Reached/sec' AND object_name LIKE '%Broker Activation%'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0
conditions.migrationNote: Task limit saturation means the MAX_QUEUE_READERS setting is too low for the message volume.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Service Broker Health' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS transmission_errors FROM sys.transmission_queue WHERE is_conversation_error = 1
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0
conditions.migrationNote: Transmission queue errors indicate Service Broker conversation failures — messages are stranded.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Service Broker Health' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT CAST(value_in_use AS INT) AS common_criteria_enabled FROM sys.configurations WHERE name = 'common criteria compliance enabled'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when = 1 (should be 0 unless mandated by compliance)
conditions.migrationNote: Enabling Common Criteria Compliance can dramatically degrade performance on Enterprise/Datacenter due to last successful login tracking on every connection.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Configuration Drift' custom metric group. Low severity.

conditions.ssConditionName: High Performance Power Plan Not Enabled
conditions.migrationNote: Requires WMI to read Windows Power Plan — no T-SQL equivalent.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Enforce via Group Policy (PowerCfg /setactive SCHEME_MIN) applied to all SQL Server OU members. Accept this monitoring gap in Redgate Monitor.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT parent_node_id) AS NumaNodeCount FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WITH (NOLOCK) WHERE parent_node_id <> 32 AND [status] = N'VISIBLE ONLINE'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when < 2 on multi-socket hardware — a count of 1 means NUMA is disabled in BIOS or not exposed to SQL Server
conditions.migrationNote: Returns distinct NUMA node count (excluding DAC node 32). On a 2-socket server this should be >= 2; a value of 1 indicates NUMA is disabled. Previous manifest entry incorrectly targeted Soft-NUMA configuration (numa_node_id = 255 filter) — corrected to use the original SQL Sentry query which counts distinct parent NUMA nodes.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Configuration Drift' custom metric group. Low severity — best practice, not an emergency. Note: alert direction is BELOW threshold (value < 2 on multi-socket).

conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry internal service health check — not applicable in Redgate Monitor.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Redgate Monitor has built-in agent health monitoring. No equivalent condition required.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT name, compatibility_level FROM sys.databases WHERE compatibility_level < (SELECT compatibility_level FROM sys.databases WHERE name = 'model') AND state_desc = 'ONLINE' AND name NOT IN ('master','msdb','tempdb')
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when any row returned (review intentional exceptions)
conditions.migrationNote: Databases running at an old compat level miss cardinality estimator improvements. Exceptions for legacy applications should be documented and acknowledged.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Configuration Drift' custom metric group. Low severity.

conditions.ssConditionName: Sleeping Session With Long Open Transaction
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS SleepingWithOpenTx FROM sys.dm_exec_sessions s JOIN sys.dm_tran_session_transactions st ON s.session_id = st.session_id JOIN sys.dm_tran_active_transactions t ON st.transaction_id = t.transaction_id WHERE s.status = 'sleeping' AND DATEDIFF(MINUTE, t.transaction_begin_time, GETDATE()) >= 10
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0 (any sleeping session with open transaction for >= 10 minutes)
conditions.migrationNote: Sleeping sessions with open transactions prevent log truncation and cause version store bloat under RCSI/snapshot isolation. Original entry returned a table (one row per session) — corrected to return a scalar count so Redgate Monitor can threshold it directly.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Session Activity' custom metric group. Medium severity.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT CAST(SUM(CAST(size AS BIGINT)) * 8.0 / 1024.0 / 1024.0 AS DECIMAL(18,2)) AS TotalDataFileSizeGB FROM sys.master_files WHERE type_desc = N'ROWS'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Use rate-of-change alerting: Low > 0.5 GB, Medium > 2 GB, High > 10 GB growth per 15-minute collection
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry uses its internal PerformanceAnalysisSqlFile history table to detect per-file growth on files >= 5 GB. Redgate Monitor proxy tracks aggregate data file size with rate-of-change. Only detects total growth across all files, not per-file or per-database. Pair with disk space alert.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'File & Storage' custom metric group. Enable rate-of-change alerting. Low severity — informational capacity planning indicator.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT cntr_value AS SqlCompilationsPerSec FROM sys.dm_os_performance_counters WHERE [object_name] LIKE N'%SQL Statistics%' AND counter_name = N'SQL Compilations/sec'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 1500 (Medium) or > 3000 (High). Correlate manually with the built-in CPU alert — Redgate Monitor cannot compound two metrics into a single alert.
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry fires this compound condition only when both CPU utilisation AND compilations/sec are simultaneously elevated. Redgate Monitor has no native compound-condition alerting. Deploy this custom metric alongside the built-in CPU alert and correlate using the Analysis graph. Same T-SQL as SS-ADV-016 — reuse that metric definition rather than creating a duplicate.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Reuse the SS-ADV-016 compilation metric. True compound alerting is not possible in Redgate Monitor — accept reduced fidelity.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT AVG(runnable_tasks_count) AS AvgRunnableTasksCount FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WITH (NOLOCK) WHERE [status] = N'VISIBLE ONLINE'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 3 (Medium) or > 10 (High) as proxy for CPU scheduling pressure. Critical SS threshold was 7,500 context switches/sec/core.
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry reads System: Context Switches/sec via Windows WMI — not accessible via T-SQL. Redgate Monitor's Activity Graph may surface this WMI counter natively but cannot alert on it directly. T-SQL proxy uses runnable tasks count as a downstream indicator of CPU scheduling pressure. Same proxy as SS-ADV-018 (High Runnable Tasks Count) — reuse that metric with appropriate threshold bands.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Reuse SS-ADV-018 runnable tasks metric. Configure Medium (> 3) and High (> 10) threshold bands to cover both High Context Switches and the Warning variant (SS-ADV-050).

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT AVG(runnable_tasks_count) AS AvgRunnableTasksCount FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WITH (NOLOCK) WHERE [status] = N'VISIBLE ONLINE'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 3 (Medium). Warning threshold in SS was 5,000 context switches/sec/core. Reuse the same metric as SS-ADV-049 with a lower Medium band.
conditions.migrationNote: Warning-level variant of SS-ADV-049. Configure as the Medium threshold band on the same Runnable Tasks custom metric. No separate metric deployment required.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Combined with SS-ADV-049 — configure Low/Medium/High threshold bands on a single 'Runnable Tasks' custom metric.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT ISNULL((SELECT MAX(COALESCE(RS.log_send_rate / NULLIF(RS.redo_rate, 0), 0)) FROM sys.dm_hadr_database_replica_states AS RS INNER JOIN sys.availability_groups AS AG ON AG.group_id = RS.group_id INNER JOIN sys.availability_replicas AS AR ON RS.group_id = AR.group_id AND RS.replica_id = AR.replica_id INNER JOIN sys.dm_hadr_availability_group_states AS AGS ON AGS.group_id = AG.group_id INNER JOIN sys.dm_hadr_availability_replica_states AS ARS ON AR.replica_id = ARS.replica_id WHERE ARS.role_desc = N'SECONDARY' AND AGS.primary_replica = @@SERVERNAME AND RS.log_send_rate > 0 AND RS.redo_rate > 0), 0) AS MaxRedoCompletionTimeSec
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 120 seconds (Low), > 300 seconds (Medium), > 600 seconds (High). Returns 0 when no AG secondaries exist.
conditions.migrationNote: Estimated redo catch-up time = log_send_rate / redo_rate. Run on the primary replica only — returns 0 on secondaries. High values mean the secondary redo thread is the bottleneck (typically I/O latency on secondary disk). Consolidate with SS-ADV-001 and SS-ADV-002 into the Replication Queue group.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Replication Queue' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT AVG(runnable_tasks_count) AS AvgRunnableTasksCount FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WITH (NOLOCK) WHERE [status] = N'VISIBLE ONLINE'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 3 (Medium) or > 10 (High) as proxy. Check Activity Graph for Hyper-V guest CPU counters if VMware Tools / Hyper-V integration services are installed.
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry reads Hyper-V Hypervisor Virtual Processor: CPU Wait Time Per Dispatch via WMI — not accessible via T-SQL. When Hyper-V cannot schedule vCPUs, SQL Server runnable tasks accumulate as a downstream indicator. Reuses same proxy as SS-ADV-049. For genuine Hyper-V vCPU wait time, collect Hyper-V PerfMon counters from the host or guest OS.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Reuse the SS-ADV-049 / SS-ADV-018 runnable tasks metric — no separate deployment needed.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT CAST(SUM(CAST(size AS BIGINT)) * 8.0 / 1024.0 / 1024.0 AS DECIMAL(18,2)) AS TotalLogFileSizeGB FROM sys.master_files WHERE type_desc = N'LOG'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Use rate-of-change alerting: Low > 0.25 GB, Medium > 1 GB, High > 5 GB growth per 15-minute collection
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry detects per-log-file growth from PerformanceAnalysisSqlFile history for files >= 1 GB. Redgate Monitor proxy tracks aggregate log size with rate-of-change. Frequent growth creates excessive VLFs — pair with SS-ADV-022 (High VLF Count).
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'File & Storage' custom metric group. Enable rate-of-change alerting.

conditions.ssConditionName: SQL Server Process Physical Memory Low
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT CAST(process_physical_memory_low AS INT) AS ProcessPhysicalMemoryLow FROM sys.dm_os_process_memory WITH (NOLOCK)
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert immediately when = 1
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Server is actively responding to a Windows low physical memory notification — buffer pool is being shrunk. Pair with SS-ADV-027 (Low PLE) and SS-ADV-074 (Windows Low Memory Notification). Common cause: max server memory set too high, leaving insufficient RAM for the OS.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Memory Pressure' custom metric group. High severity — buffer pool shrinkage causes immediate performance degradation.

conditions.ssConditionName: SQL Server Process Virtual Memory Low
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT CAST(process_virtual_memory_low AS INT) AS ProcessVirtualMemoryLow FROM sys.dm_os_process_memory WITH (NOLOCK)
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert immediately when = 1 (treat as High regardless of SS default — virtual memory low on 64-bit is a critical emergency)
conditions.migrationNote: On 64-bit SQL Server, virtual address space exhaustion is extremely rare and almost always caused by CLR components, linked servers, in-memory OLTP, or oversized sort operations consuming non-buffer-pool memory. Requires immediate investigation.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Memory Pressure' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS ActiveCorruptPages FROM msdb.dbo.suspect_pages WHERE event_type IN (1, 2, 3)
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert immediately when > 0
conditions.migrationNote: Event types: 1 = 823 error (hard I/O error), 2 = 824 error (logical consistency), 3 = torn page. Any non-zero count requires immediate DBCC CHECKDB on affected databases. The original SS query returned a table grouped by database — this T-SQL returns a scalar count for Redgate Monitor compatibility.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate all three Suspect Pages conditions into a 'Suspect Pages Health' custom metric group. This is the highest severity of the three.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS SuspectPageRowCount FROM msdb.dbo.suspect_pages
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 100 (Low), > 500 (Medium), > 900 (High — table approaching 1,000-row hard limit after which new events are silently dropped)
conditions.migrationNote: msdb.dbo.suspect_pages is capped at 1,000 rows. Once full, new corruption events go unrecorded. A count near 900 means the table must be reviewed and archived. A high count also indicates persistent I/O hardware issues on this server.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Suspect Pages Health' custom metric group.

conditions.ssConditionName: Suspect Pages — Increase in Fixed Pages
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS FixedSuspectPages FROM msdb.dbo.suspect_pages WHERE event_type IN (4, 5, 7)
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Use rate-of-change alerting: alert on any increase (event type 4 = DBCC repair, 5 = automatic page repair via AG/mirroring, 7 = deallocated by DBCC)
conditions.migrationNote: An increase means pages were corrected by DBCC or automatic page repair. The root cause (bad disk, SAN issue, memory error) almost certainly persists and will produce new corruption. The original SS query returned a table grouped by database — this returns a scalar for Redgate Monitor compatibility.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Suspect Pages Health' custom metric group. Enable rate-of-change alerting.

conditions.customMetricSQL: DECLARE @cpuCount INT, @tempdbFiles INT, @recommended INT; SELECT @cpuCount = COUNT(*) FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WHERE [status] = N'VISIBLE ONLINE' AND is_online = 1; SELECT @tempdbFiles = COUNT(*) FROM sys.master_files WHERE database_id = 2 AND type_desc = N'ROWS'; SET @recommended = CASE WHEN @cpuCount <= 8 THEN @cpuCount ELSE 8 END; SELECT (@recommended - @tempdbFiles) AS TempdbFileDeficit;
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0 (fewer TempDB data files than recommended). Covers servers with up to 8 logical cores — see SS-ADV-064 for > 8 cores.
conditions.migrationNote: The original SS condition evaluated two separate result sets internally. Combined into a single scalar for Redgate Monitor. In SQL Server 2016+, TF 1117 and 1118 are on by default for TempDB, eliminating some legacy contention scenarios — but file count still matters for allocation bitmap contention.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate with SS-ADV-064 into 'TempDB Health' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT CAST(SUM(version_store_reserved_page_count) * 8.0 / 1024.0 AS DECIMAL(18,2)) AS VersionStoreSizeMB FROM tempdb.sys.dm_db_file_space_usage
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 100 MB (Low), > 512 MB (Medium), > 1024 MB (High)
conditions.migrationNote: Large version store is caused by long-running transactions under RCSI or snapshot isolation preventing cleanup. Identify the blocking transaction via sys.dm_tran_active_snapshot_database_transactions. SQL Sentry used internal collection; T-SQL queries tempdb.sys.dm_db_file_space_usage directly.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'TempDB Health' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT CAST(100.0 * SUM(unallocated_extent_page_count) / NULLIF(SUM(total_page_count), 0) AS DECIMAL(10,2)) AS TempdbUnallocatedPct FROM tempdb.sys.dm_db_file_space_usage
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when < 20% (Low), < 10% (Medium), < 5% (High) — alert direction is BELOW threshold
conditions.migrationNote: A sudden drop in TempDB free space often indicates a runaway sort, hash join, or RCSI version store growth. Alert threshold direction is below (low value = problem). A sudden drop often correlates with a runaway sort, hash join, or RCSI version store growth event.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'TempDB Health' custom metric group. Alert direction is below threshold — configure Redgate Monitor accordingly.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT size) AS TempdbUniqueFileSizeCount FROM sys.master_files WHERE database_id = 2 AND type_desc = N'ROWS'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 1 (any files of different size breaks round-robin allocation)
conditions.migrationNote: Unequal TempDB file sizes break round-robin allocation, causing all allocation to concentrate on the first file and creating SGAM/GAM contention. In SQL Server 2016+, TF 1117 is on by default ensuring auto-growth is equal — but initial sizing must still be equal.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'TempDB Health' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: DECLARE @cpuCount INT, @tempdbFiles INT; SELECT @cpuCount = COUNT(*) FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WHERE [status] = N'VISIBLE ONLINE' AND is_online = 1; SELECT @tempdbFiles = COUNT(*) FROM sys.master_files WHERE database_id = 2 AND type_desc = N'ROWS'; SELECT CASE WHEN @cpuCount <= 8 THEN ABS(@cpuCount - @tempdbFiles) ELSE 0 END AS TempdbCpuFileDelta;
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0 on servers with <= 8 logical cores (file count should equal core count). Returns 0 on > 8 core servers — handled by SS-ADV-064.
conditions.migrationNote: The original SS condition evaluated two separate result sets (CPU count and file count) internally. Combined into a single scalar delta for Redgate Monitor. Returns 0 on high-core-count servers to avoid double-alerting with SS-ADV-064.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate with SS-ADV-064 into 'TempDB Health' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: DECLARE @cpuCount INT, @tempdbFiles INT, @minRecommended INT; SELECT @cpuCount = COUNT(*) FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WHERE [status] = N'VISIBLE ONLINE' AND is_online = 1; SELECT @tempdbFiles = COUNT(*) FROM sys.master_files WHERE database_id = 2 AND type_desc = N'ROWS'; SET @minRecommended = CASE WHEN @cpuCount <= 8 THEN 0 WHEN @cpuCount / 4 > 8 THEN @cpuCount / 4 ELSE 8 END; SELECT CASE WHEN @cpuCount > 8 AND (@minRecommended - @tempdbFiles) > 0 THEN @minRecommended - @tempdbFiles ELSE 0 END AS TempdbFileDeficit;
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0 on servers with > 8 logical cores (minimum recommended = max(8, CPU_count / 4)). Returns 0 on <= 8 core servers.
conditions.migrationNote: For high-core-count servers, minimum TempDB files = max(8, CPU_count / 4). Returns 0 on <= 8 core servers to avoid double-alerting with SS-ADV-063. Uses CASE instead of GREATEST() for SQL Server 2016–2019 compatibility.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate with SS-ADV-063 into 'TempDB Health' custom metric group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT MAX(CASE WHEN fs.num_of_reads + fs.num_of_writes = 0 THEN 0 ELSE CAST(fs.io_stall AS DECIMAL(18,2)) / (fs.num_of_reads + fs.num_of_writes) END) AS MaxAvgIOLatencyMs FROM sys.dm_io_virtual_file_stats(NULL, NULL) AS fs INNER JOIN sys.master_files AS mf ON fs.database_id = mf.database_id AND fs.[file_id] = mf.[file_id]
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 10 ms (Low), > 20 ms (Medium), > 50 ms (High) as proxy for Tintri throttle impact
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry reads Tintri Datastore: Throttle Latency (ms) via its Tintri storage array integration — not accessible via T-SQL. T-SQL proxy measures SQL Server observed I/O latency as a downstream indicator: if Tintri is throttling, SQL Server will observe elevated read/write latency. For genuine Tintri datastore throttle monitoring, use the Tintri VMstore REST API or Tintri-native tooling. Same metric as SS-ADV-020 (High Disk Waits and Latency) — use that direct alert as the primary alert.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Use SS-ADV-020 (direct I/O latency alert) for all three Tintri conditions. Tintri-specific attribution is not possible via T-SQL.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT MAX(CASE WHEN fs.num_of_writes = 0 THEN 0 ELSE CAST(fs.io_stall_write_ms AS DECIMAL(18,2)) / fs.num_of_writes END) AS MaxAvgWriteLatencyMs FROM sys.dm_io_virtual_file_stats(NULL, NULL) AS fs INNER JOIN sys.master_files AS mf ON fs.database_id = mf.database_id AND fs.[file_id] = mf.[file_id]
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 5 ms (Low), > 10 ms (Medium), > 50 ms (High) as proxy — transaction log targets: < 2 ms ideal, > 10 ms investigate
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry reads Tintri Virtual Disk: Throttle Latency (ms) via Tintri integration — not accessible via T-SQL. Proxy measures max write latency across all database files. For genuine Tintri vDisk monitoring, use Tintri's management API or native monitoring.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate all three Tintri conditions — use SS-ADV-020 / SS-ADV-065 I/O latency metric as the common proxy.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT SUM(fs.io_stall) AS TotalIOStallMs FROM sys.dm_io_virtual_file_stats(NULL, NULL) AS fs INNER JOIN sys.master_files AS mf ON fs.database_id = mf.database_id AND fs.[file_id] = mf.[file_id] WHERE mf.state_desc = N'ONLINE'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Use rate-of-change alerting: alert when total I/O stall grows > 30,000 ms/interval (Medium) or > 120,000 ms/interval (High). io_stall is cumulative — rate-of-change required.
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry reads Tintri Virtual Machine: Throttle Latency (ms) via Tintri integration — not accessible via T-SQL. VM-level throttling affects all files simultaneously, so aggregate I/O stall rate-of-change is the best proxy. For genuine Tintri VM throttle monitoring, use Tintri's management API.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate all three Tintri conditions — use SS-ADV-020 / SS-ADV-065 as the common proxy. Enable rate-of-change for VM-level throttle detection.

conditions.ssConditionName: Trace Flags — Number Turned On Changed
conditions.customMetricSQL: CREATE TABLE #tf (TraceFlag INT, [Status] INT, [Global] INT, [Session] INT); INSERT INTO #tf EXEC ('DBCC TRACESTATUS WITH NO_INFOMSGS'); SELECT COUNT(*) AS EnabledTraceFlagCount FROM #tf WHERE [Status] = 1; DROP TABLE #tf;
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Use rate-of-change alerting: alert on any change in the enabled trace flag count
conditions.migrationNote: Returns count of trace flags currently enabled (Status = 1). Rate-of-change detects unexpected activation or deactivation. Common production trace flags (SQL 2016 and earlier): TF 1117, 1118 (TempDB), 2371 (dynamic statistics), 4199 (QP hotfixes). In SQL 2016+, TF 1117 and 1118 are defaults for TempDB — do not add them manually.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate with SS-ADV-069 into a 'Trace Flag Monitoring' group. Both use DBCC TRACESTATUS — reuse a single collection.

conditions.customMetricSQL: CREATE TABLE #tf2 (TraceFlag INT, [Status] INT, [Global] INT, [Session] INT); INSERT INTO #tf2 EXEC ('DBCC TRACESTATUS WITH NO_INFOMSGS'); SELECT COUNT(*) AS TotalTraceFlagCount FROM #tf2; DROP TABLE #tf2;
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Use rate-of-change alerting: alert on any change (trace flag added or removed regardless of status)
conditions.migrationNote: Broader than SS-ADV-068 — catches trace flags being added or removed even if their status hasn't changed. Session-level trace flags (set without -1) only appear for the current session. Startup trace flags (set via -T in service startup parameters) always appear.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate with SS-ADV-068 into 'Trace Flag Monitoring' group.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT CAST(process_physical_memory_low AS INT) AS PhysicalMemoryLow FROM sys.dm_os_process_memory WITH (NOLOCK)
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when = 1 (flag is set — Windows / VMware balloon driver is reclaiming physical memory from SQL Server)
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry reads VMware balloon driver metrics (mem.vmmemctl) via VMware vCenter integration — not accessible via T-SQL. From SQL Server's perspective, VMware ballooning is indistinguishable from a physical memory shortage. Proxy uses the same flag as SS-ADV-054. For genuine ballooning monitoring, use VMware vSphere performance counters (mem.vmmemctl) or esxtop.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Reuse SS-ADV-054 (SQL Server Process Physical Memory Low) — same proxy metric. Configure VMware Ballooning as an annotation in the migration notes only.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT AVG(runnable_tasks_count) AS AvgRunnableTasksCount FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WITH (NOLOCK) WHERE [status] = N'VISIBLE ONLINE'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 3 (Medium) or > 10 (High) as proxy. VMware co-stop > 3% is the critical threshold in vSphere.
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry reads VMware cpu.costop counter via VMware integration — not accessible via T-SQL. Co-scheduling constraints prevent vCPU execution, which causes SQL Server runnable tasks to accumulate. Proxy is the same runnable tasks metric as SS-ADV-018/SS-ADV-049. For genuine co-stop monitoring, use esxtop or VMware vSphere performance counters.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Reuse SS-ADV-018 / SS-ADV-049 runnable tasks metric. All VMware CPU and Hyper-V proxy conditions share this metric — no separate deployment needed.

conditions.ssConditionName: VMware High Ready Time % per vCPU
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT AVG(runnable_tasks_count) AS AvgRunnableTasksCount FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WITH (NOLOCK) WHERE [status] = N'VISIBLE ONLINE'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 5 (Medium) or > 10 (High) as proxy. VMware critical threshold: cpu.ready > 10% per vCPU.
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry reads VMware cpu.ready counter per vCPU via VMware integration — not accessible via T-SQL. vCPUs waiting for hypervisor scheduling cause SQL Server runnable tasks to grow. Proxy is the same runnable tasks metric as SS-ADV-018/SS-ADV-049. For genuine Ready Time monitoring, use VMware vSphere performance counters (cpu.ready) or esxtop.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Reuse SS-ADV-018 / SS-ADV-049 runnable tasks metric with an additional High threshold band (> 10).

conditions.ssConditionName: VMware High Ready Time % per vCPU — Warning
conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT AVG(runnable_tasks_count) AS AvgRunnableTasksCount FROM sys.dm_os_schedulers WITH (NOLOCK) WHERE [status] = N'VISIBLE ONLINE'
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 3 (Medium). VMware warning threshold: cpu.ready > 5% per vCPU.
conditions.migrationNote: Warning-level variant of SS-ADV-072. Configure as the Medium threshold band on the same Runnable Tasks custom metric. No separate metric deployment required.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Combined with SS-ADV-072 — single runnable tasks metric with Low/Medium/High threshold bands covers High Context Switches, Hyper-V, VMware Co-Stop, VMware Ready Time Warning and Critical.

conditions.customMetricSQL: SELECT COUNT(*) AS LowMemoryNotificationCount FROM (SELECT CAST(orb.record AS XML) AS xmlRec FROM sys.dm_os_ring_buffers AS orb CROSS JOIN sys.dm_os_sys_info AS osi WHERE orb.ring_buffer_type = N'RING_BUFFER_RESOURCE_MONITOR' AND DATEADD(SECOND, -CAST(((osi.cpu_ticks / (osi.cpu_ticks / osi.ms_ticks)) - orb.timestamp) / 1000 AS INT), GETDATE()) > DATEADD(MINUTE, -6, GETDATE())) AS rb CROSS APPLY rb.xmlRec.nodes('Record') AS rec(x) WHERE rec.x.value('(ResourceMonitor/IndicatorsSystem)[1]', 'TINYINT') = 2
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 0 (Medium) or > 3 (High) notifications within the last 6 minutes
conditions.migrationNote: IndicatorsSystem = 2 corresponds to RESOURCE_MEMPHYSICAL_LOW — Windows signalled SQL Server that physical memory is critically low. Each notification causes SQL Server to shrink the buffer pool, causing PLE to drop and disk I/O to spike. Pair with SS-ADV-027 (Low PLE) and SS-ADV-054 (Physical Memory Low flag) for a complete memory pressure picture.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Consolidate into 'Memory Pressure' custom metric group. High severity — active Windows memory pressure is a critical condition.

conditions.customMetricSQL: CREATE TABLE #err_log_size (ArchiveNo INT, CreateDate NVARCHAR(128), Size INT); INSERT INTO #err_log_size EXEC master.dbo.sp_enumerrorlogs; SELECT TOP 1 CAST(Size AS DECIMAL(18,2)) / 1024.0 / 1024.0 AS ErrorLogSizeMB FROM #err_log_size ORDER BY ArchiveNo ASC; DROP TABLE #err_log_size;
conditions.customMetricThreshold: Alert when > 50 MB (Low), > 100 MB (Medium), > 500 MB (High)
conditions.migrationNote: SQL Sentry uses this as a gate condition in composite rules (e.g., High Number of Failed Logins) to prevent slow queries against very large log files. In Redgate Monitor, deploy as a standalone informational metric. A large error log indicates excessive error activity — investigate the root cause. Cycle via EXEC sp_cycle_errorlog during maintenance.
conditions.simplificationStrategy: Standalone low-priority maintenance metric. Size in MB returned from sp_enumerrorlogs (sp_enumerrorlogs.Size column is in bytes — divide by 1024 twice).
